text: The chemical activation and hydrogenation of CO2 into methanol represent a promising strategy for the utilization of captured carbon. In this study, we investigate the performance of size-selected $Cu_4$ clusters supported on alumina thin films for $CO_2$ reduction. Catalytic activity was evaluated under near-atmospheric pressures with low $CO_2$ concentrations, while the oxidation states of the clusters were monitored via in situ grazing incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy. Our findings demonstrate that $Cu_4$ clusters are exceptionally active, representing the most efficient catalyst reported to date for $CO_2$ conversion to $CH_3OH$ at low pressures. Theoretical analysis using density functional theory suggests that these clusters significantly lower the activation barrier for methanol synthesis. These results highlight the potential of sub-nanometer copper clusters as highly efficient catalysts for the sustainable recycling of $CO_2$.
